content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 12, 2017

Wendy L. House
San Marcos City Attorney
Lounsbery Ferguson
Altona & Peak, LLP
960 Canterbury Place, Suite 300
Escondido, CA 92025-3870

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-17-228

Dear Ms. House:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. May a City Council member who has a conflict of interest involving one or more applicants to a City board or commission remain on the dais during the statement/question period involving the applicants, provided the Council member does not participate in any conversations?

2. If the City's process is to hear all applicant statements prior to making any nominations, may the potentially interested Council member be present for the statement/question period

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-17-228
Page No. 2

involving any of the other applicants for which the interested Council member does not have a
disqualifying conflict of interest?

CONCLUSION

1. & 2. A Council member with a conflict must: following the announcement of the agenda
item and prior to any discussion of the item publicly identify the conflict, recuse himself or herself,
and leave the room until the applicants in question are nominated and votes are cast.³

FACTS

The City has a long-standing policy regarding the City Council's appointment of applicants
to various City boards, committees and commissions serving as advisory bodies to the City Council.
The City's policy states that applicants are to appear at a City Council meeting prior to their
nomination and consideration for an appointed position.

During this appearance, the applicants generally provide a statement to the Council about
their qualifications for and interest in the position, and answer any questions the Council might
have at that time. At the conclusion of the statement/question period, the Council then makes the
nominations and votes on the appointments.

However, you seek clarification regarding when a Council member who may have a
potential conflict as to one candidate in an applicant pool must leave the dais prior to the
nomination of that applicant to any position, and whether the Council member may remain at the
dais for the introduction and/or nomination of other candidates.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions help to ensure that public officials perform their
duties impartially, free from bias attributable to their own financial interests or those of persons
who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, the Act prohibits a public official from
making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of
the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one
or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)

In appointing individuals to City boards, committees and commissions, Council members
are making governmental decisions. Regulation 18704(a) provides that a public official makes a
governmental decision when he or she appoints a person.⁴

___________________________
³ A Council member still prohibited from making, participating in, or influencing the appointment decision if it
is inextricably interlinked to an appointment decision in which he or she has a conflict of interest. Where the various
appointment decisions may be separated into distinct decisions consistent with the rules for segmentation in Regulation
18706, the official may participate when there is no longer a financial interest.

File No. I-17-228
Page No. 3


Method of Recusal

When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including city council members) has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must by following the announcement of the agenda item, but prior to the discussion of the item: (1) orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as well as details of the interest, on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Regulation 18707.)

For the situation described, because the Council member must comply with the above procedure immediately following the announcement of the agenda item, and remain out of the room until the completion of the agenda item, the Council member may not stay on the dais for any part of the discussion or deliberations of that agenda item.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl


⁴ An official's appointment decision, like any other governmental decision, should be examined independently to determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the appointment will have a material financial effect on the official's interests

1. & 2. A Council member with a conflict must: following the announcement of the agenda 
item and prior to any discussion of the item publicly identify the conflict, recuse himself or herself, 
and leave the room until the applicants in question are nominated and votes are cast.3
sections.facts:
The City has a long-standing policy regarding the City Council's appointment of applicants 
to various City boards, committees and commissions serving as advisory bodies to the City Council. 
The City's policy states that applicants are to appear at a City Council meeting prior to their 
nomination and consideration for an appointed position. 
During this appearance, the applicants generally provide a statement to the Council about 
their qualifications for and interest in the position, and answer any questions the Council might 
have at that time. At the conclusion of the statement/question period, the Council then makes the 
nominations and votes on the appointments. 
However, you seek clarification regarding when a Council member who may have a 
potential conflict as to one candidate in an applicant pool must leave the dais prior to the 
nomination of that applicant to any position, and whether the Council member may remain at the 
dais for the introduction and/or nomination of other candidates.
sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict of interest provisions help to ensure that public officials perfonn their 
duties impartially, free from bias attributable to their own financial interests or those of persons 
who have supported them. (Section 8100l(b).) Specifically, the Act prohibits a public official from 
making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental 
decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) 
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of 
the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation I 8700(a).) 
In appointing individuals to City boards, committees and commissions, Council members 
are making governmental decisions. Regulation I 8704(a) provides that a public official makes a 
governmental decision when he or she appoints a person.4 
3 A Council member still prohibited from making, participating in, or influencing the appointment decision if it 
is inextricably interlinked to an appointment decision in which he or she has a conflict of interest. Where the various 
appointment decisions may be separated into distinct decisions consistent with the rules for segmentation in Regulation 
18706, the official may participate when there is no longer a financial interest. 


Method of Recusal  
When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including 
city council members) has a conflict ofinterest in a decision noticed at a public meetingr he 
or she must be following the announcement of the agenda item, but prior to the discussion of the 
item: (I) orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as well as details of the 
interest, on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the 
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Regulation 18707.) 
For the situation described, because the Council member must comply with the above 
procedure immediately following the announcement of the agenda item, and remain out of the room 
until the completion of the agenda item, the Council member may not stay on the dais for any part 
of the discussion or deliberations of that agenda item.
